title: plan a pentest methodology for a web application

messages.content: plan a pentest methodology for a web application

**Web Application Penetration Testing Methodology** Below is a comprehensive, step‑by‑step methodology you can follow (or adapt) for a structured web‑app pentest. Each phase includes objectives, typical activities, tools, and deliverables to ensure repeatability and thorough coverage. --- ## 1. Pre‑Engagement & Planning | Activity | Description | Output | |----------|-------------|--------| | **Scope Definition** | Identify in‑scope URLs, subdomains, APIs, technologies, and any out‑of‑scope assets. Clarify testing type (black‑box, gray‑box, white‑box) and rules of engagement (time windows, DoS limits, credentials). | Scope document, Rules of Engagement (RoE) | | **Legal & Authorization** | Obtain written authorization, NDAs, and any required compliance sign‑offs. | Signed contract / authorization letter | | **Objective Setting** | Define goals (e.g., OWASP Top 10 coverage, business‑logic flaws, data exfiltration, privilege escalation). | Test objectives list | | **Logistics** | Determine reporting format, communication channels, escalation contacts, and deliverable timelines. | Project plan & contact matrix | --- ## 2. Information Gathering (Reconnaissance) | Sub‑phase | Techniques | Tools / Commands | |-----------|------------|------------------| | **Passive Recon** | • WHOIS, DNS enumeration
• SSL/TLS certificate transparency logs
• Public code repositories (GitHub, GitLab)
• Search engine dorking
• Social media / job postings | `whois`, `dig`, `sublist3r`, `amass`, `crt.sh`, `Google dorks`, `GitHub API`, `theHarvester` | | **Active Recon** | • Port/service scanning (focus on 80/443, but also 8080, 8443, etc.)
• Technology fingerprinting (web server, frameworks, CMS)
• Subdomain brute‑force / DNS zone transfer attempts (if permitted)
• Crawling for hidden directories/files | `nmap`, `masscan`, `whatweb`, `Wappalyzer`, `gobuster`, `dirsearch`, `ffuf`, `aquatone` | | **Asset Mapping** | Build a spreadsheet or diagram of all discovered endpoints, parameters, APIs, third‑party services, and authentication mechanisms. | Asset inventory (CSV/Excel) + visual map (draw.io, Lucidchart) | *Deliverable:* **Reconnaissance Report** – list of assets, technologies, and initial observations. --- ## 3. Threat Modeling & Attack Surface Analysis | Activity | Description | |----------|-------------| | **Identify Entry Points** | Login pages, registration, password reset, file upload, API endpoints, webhooks, admin panels. | | **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** | Sketch how data moves: user → browser → app server → DB → third‑party services. Highlight trust boundaries. | | **Identify Trust Levels & Privileges** | Map roles (anonymous, user, admin, service accounts) and their permissions. | | **Prioritize** | Use STRIDE or PASTA to rank threats based on impact & likelihood. | | **Deliverable:** Threat model document (DFD + risk ranking). | --- ## 4. Vulnerability Identification (Scanning & Manual Testing) ### 4.1 Automated Scanning | Category | Tools | Typical Command / Usage | |----------|-------|--------------------------| | **Network/Service** | `nmap` scripts (`http-vuln*`, `ssl-enum-ciphers`) | `nmap -sV --script http-vuln* ` | | **Web App Scanner** | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite Professional, Nikto, Acunetix, Nessus Web App module | `zap-baseline.py -t https://example.com -r zap-report.html` | | **Dependency/Component** | `npm audit`, `pip-audit`, `bundler-audit`, `Retire.js`, `Snyk` | `npm audit` | | **Configuration** | `sslscan`, `testssl.sh`, `securityheaders.com` API | `testssl.sh example.com` | *Tip:* Run scans in **safe mode** first (no aggressive payloads) to avoid DoS; then optionally run aggressive scans if permitted. ### 4.2 Manual Testing (OWASP Top 10 Focus) | Vulnerability Class | Key Tests | Manual Techniques / Tools | |---------------------|-----------|----------------------------| | **Injection** (SQL, NoSQL, OS Command) | Parameter fuzzing, error‑based, blind, time‑based | Burp Intruder, sqlmap, manual payloads (`' OR 1=1--`) | | **Broken Authentication** | Credential stuffing, brute force, session fixation, password reset abuse | Burp Intruder, Hydra, custom scripts | | **Sensitive Data Exposure** | Check for missing TLS, weak ciphers, cache‑able sensitive data, debug info | Browser devtools, `curl -I`, `openssl s_client` | | **XML External Entities (XXE)** | Upload XML with external entities, test SOAP/REST XML endpoints | Burp Scanner, manual XML payloads | | **Broken Access Control** | Horizontal/vertical privilege escalation, IDOR, forced browsing | Burp Suite’s Autorun, manual parameter tampering | | **Security Misconfiguration** | Default accounts, directory listing, verbose error messages, unnecessary HTTP methods | Nikto, manual dir browsing, `OPTIONS` method probing | | **Cross‑Site Scripting (XSS)** | Reflected, stored, DOM‑based; test via URL params, headers, user‑agent | Burp Scanner, XSSHunter, manual payloads (`
• Title
• CVSS score & vector
• Description
• Location (URL, parameter)
• Steps to reproduce
• Proof‑of‑concept (screenshots, logs)
• Risk rating
• Remediation guidance | | **Appendices** | Tool output logs, raw scan files, threat model diagram, asset inventory, timeline. | | **Remediation Prioritization** | Suggested fix order (critical → low) with estimated effort. | | **Sign‑off** | Confirmation that testing is complete and all findings have been communicated. | *Format:* PDF (primary) + optional HTML/web‑based report for easy navigation. Include a **remediation checklist** and **retest plan**. --- ## 7. Cleanup & Retest | Action | Description | |--------|-------------| | **Remove Artifacts** | Delete any uploaded shells, test files, or temporary accounts created during testing. | | **Restore State** | If any configuration changes were made (e.g., enabling debug mode), revert them. | | **Retest** | After client patches, verify that vulnerabilities are resolved (optional but recommended). | | **Final Sign‑off** | Obtain client acknowledgment of remediation and retest results. | --- ## 8. Continuous Improvement - **Lessons Learned Meeting** – discuss what worked, what didn’t, and update internal playbooks. - **Knowledge Base** – add new payloads, custom scripts, and findings to your internal repository. - **Training** – share findings with development teams to improve secure coding practices (e.g., OWASP ASVS training). --- ### Quick Reference Checklist (One‑Page) | Phase | Key Actions | |-------|-------------| | **Pre‑Engagement** | Scope, RoE, objectives, legal sign‑off | | **Recon** | Passive + active asset mapping, tech fingerprinting | | **Threat Modeling** | DFD, trust boundaries, STRIDE/PASTA prioritization | | **Scanning** | Automated (ZAP/Burp, Nessus, npm audit) + safe → aggressive if allowed | | **Manual Testing** | OWASP Top 10 focused tests (Injection, Auth, XSS, etc.) | | **Exploitation** | PoC exploits, privilege escalation, impact demo | | **Reporting** | Exec summary, detailed findings, remediation, appendices | | **Cleanup** | Remove artifacts, restore state, retest if needed | | **Improvement** | Lessons learned, update playbooks, train devs | --- ### Suggested Tool Stack (Open‑Source + Commercial Options) | Category | Open‑Source | Commercial | |----------|-------------|------------| | Proxy / Intercept | Burp Suite Community, OWASP ZAP | Burp Suite Professional | | Scanning | Nikto, Zap, Nessus (home), OpenVAS | Acunetix, Qualys Web App Scanning | | Fuzzing | ffuf, wfuzz, Burp Intruder | Burp Suite Professional (Intruder) | | SQLi | sqlmap, NoSQLMap | – | | XSS | XSSHunter, DalFox | – | | Deserialization | ysoserial, SerialBrute | – | | Dependency | npm audit, pip-audit, Retire.js | Snyk, WhiteSource | | Reporting | Dradis, MagicTree, Faraday | Custom PDF/HTML templates | | Collaboration | Jira, Confluence, Slack | – | | SIEM / Log Review | ELK Stack, Splunk Free | Splunk Enterprise, QRadar | --- **Final Note:** Tailor the depth of each phase to the agreed‑upon time budget and risk tolerance. For high‑value applications, allocate more time to manual testing and business‑logic validation; for lower‑risk or time‑boxed engagements, lean on automated scanners followed by targeted manual verification. Feel free to ask for any specific phase elaborated (e.g., sample Burp Suite Intruder config, SQLi cheat sheet, or a template report). Happy testing!
